message.content: Hey, good morning! Ready to keep working on the project?
We decided to use SQLite over Postgres because it's embedded and needs no setup. Can you make sure the schema migration handles the new columns?
Understood. SQLite is the right call here — zero infrastructure, no separate process, and it bundles perfectly with the app. I'll update the migration to add the new columns.
I prefer small, focused PRs — easier to review and less risky. Let's keep each feature on its own branch and merge separately.
Agreed. Small PRs also make it easier to bisect regressions later. I'll open a dedicated branch for each logical change.
We tracked down the bug. The bug was a race condition: two processes writing to the same cursor file without locking. Adding a file lock fixed it.
That's a classic race. File locks (flock/lockfile) serialize writes reliably. Good find.
All hook handlers in this codebase use dependency injection via interfaces for testability. Should we follow the same pattern for the new handler?
Absolutely — follow the existing pattern. Pass dependencies through the constructor as interface types; that way tests can swap in mocks without touching the implementation.
Watch out: import paths break when running from a symlinked directory — always use import.meta.url to resolve paths relative to the current file.
Good gotcha to document. Using `new URL('./foo', import.meta.url)` is safe under symlinks because it resolves against the file's real location, not the process cwd.
We fixed fire-and-forget HTTP by using socket.unref() after the request finishes. That way the Node process can exit cleanly without waiting for the response.
Correct fix. socket.unref() tells Node the socket shouldn't keep the event loop alive, so fire-and-forget requests don't block clean shutdown.
Can you summarize our PR workflow? To ship a PR: push branch, request Copilot review, fix comments, squash merge to develop.
Confirmed workflow: push branch → request Copilot review via the reviewer API → address feedback → squash merge into develop. Never merge directly to main.
